did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the YOLOv7 deep learning model for automated detection and classification of skin cancer lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma) in dermoscopic images. The study employs metaheuristic optimization algorithms (Cuckoo Search Optimization and Pelican Optimization Algorithm) to tune the hyperparameters of the YOLOv7 model, achieving specific accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and optimization of a YOLOv7 framework for skin cancer detection using Cuckoo Search and Pelican Optimization algorithms. It details specific methodologies including data collection from MED-NODE and BCC-MEL-SCC datasets, hyperparameter tuning processes, model training (50 epochs), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) comparing baseline and optimized models. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICIIET), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Optimized YOLOv7 Framework for Skin Cancer Detection using Cuckoo Search and Pelican-Based Hyperparameter Tuning
authors: Divya K Darun Adithiya V S
journal: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Innovations in Engineering and Technology (ICIIET)
brief_summary: This study proposes an optimized YOLOv7 framework for the automated detection and classification of skin cancer lesions, specifically melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma. The researchers utilized the MED-NODE and BCC-MEL-SCC public datasets, applying on-the-fly data augmentation to address class imbalance. To enhance model performance, hyperparameters were tuned independently using two metaheuristic algorithms: Cuckoo Search Optimization (CSO) and Pelican Optimization Algorithm (POA). The models were evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score metrics. The CSO-optimized model achieved the highest performance with 94.1% accuracy, while the POA-optimized model reached 93.8% accuracy, both outperforming the baseline YOLOv7 model. These results demonstrate that metaheuristic hyperparameter optimization significantly improves the diagnostic capability of YOLOv7 for real-time dermatological applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in both MED-NODE (170 cases, 70 confirmed) and BCC-MEL-SCC datasets; final augmented count 3460 images.
Included in BCC-MEL-SCC dataset; final augmented count 2491 images.
Included in BCC-MEL-SCC dataset; final augmented count 470 images.

all_task_types.target: Bounding box coordinates, objectness scores, and class probabilities for skin cancer lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper uses YOLOv7, an object detection model, to simultaneously localize and classify skin lesions in dermoscopic images. The detection head predicts bounding box coordinates, objectness confidence, and class probabilities. The loss function includes coordinate regression (MSE), objectness loss, and classification loss (cross-entropy). The task involves multi-class classification (MEL, BCC, SCC) within a detection framework.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
all_task_types.details: The model performs multi-class classification of skin lesions as part of the detection process. The paper reports classification metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score for the classes melanoma, BCC, and SCC.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE
datasets_used.description: A public repository of skin lesion images. In this study, only the melanoma (MEL) class was extracted from this dataset, consisting of 170 cases with 70 confirmed melanoma cases.

use.training: Used as a source for melanoma images; after on-the-fly data augmentation, 3460 melanoma images were generated and split into training (2768), validation (346), and testing (347) sets.
use.validation: Used for validation (346 images) after augmentation and splitting.
use.testing: Used for testing (347 images) after augmentation and splitting.

datasets_used.name: BCC-MEL-SCC
datasets_used.description: A multi-classification public dataset containing images of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Melanoma (MEL), and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC). All available images from this dataset were included in the study.

use.training: Used as a source for BCC and SCC images (and additional MEL images if present in the combined set, though the text implies MED-NODE provided the MEL count specifically). After augmentation, 2491 BCC and 470 SCC images were generated and split into training, validation, and testing sets.
use.validation: Used for validation after augmentation and splitting.
use.testing: Used for testing after augmentation and splitting.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares model performance using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score metrics without statistical significance tests.

MED-NODE and BCC-MEL-SCC (Combined).description: Results reported for the combined dataset comprising Melanoma (MEL), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) classes after on-the-fly data augmentation.
MED-NODE and BCC-MEL-SCC (Combined).notes: The CSO-optimized model achieved the highest accuracy (94.1%). The POA-optimized model achieved 93.8% accuracy. Both optimized models outperformed the baseline YOLOv7 model. The paper notes that further research on the SCC class is desirable due to its smaller sample size (470 images) compared to Melanoma (3460 images) and BCC (2491 images).

architecture_details.neck: Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP) module and Path Aggregation Network (PANet)-like feature fusion mechanism
architecture_details.head: One-stage object detection head performing localization and classification in a single forward pass
architecture_details.loss_function: Weighted sum of coordinate regression loss (λ_coord), objectness loss (λ_obj), and classification loss (λ_cls)

classification_or_output_head.task: Multi-class detection and classification of Melanoma (MEL), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)

preprocessing_techniques: On-the-fly data augmentation (rotation, flipping, and scaling) applied to address class imbalance, particularly for the underrepresented Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) class.
Stratified sampling for dataset splitting to ensure proportional representation of all classes in training, validation, and testing sets.
Random shuffling of images prior to folder assignment.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to address class imbalance, specifically for the underrepresented Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) class. Techniques included rotation, flipping, and scaling. This served as synthetic balancing to ensure the model was more general and less biased towards majority classes.

conclusions: The application of metaheuristic hyperparameter optimization algorithms, specifically Cuckoo Search Optimization (CSO) and Pelican Optimization Algorithm (POA), significantly improves the detection and classification performance of the YOLOv7 model for skin cancer lesions.

The CSO-optimized YOLOv7 model achieved the highest performance with 94.1% accuracy, 93.0% sensitivity, 94.8% specificity, 92.5% precision, and 92.7% F1-score, outperforming the baseline YOLOv7 model (89.65% accuracy).
The POA-optimized YOLOv7 model also demonstrated improved performance with 93.8% accuracy, 92.5% sensitivity, 94.5% specificity, 93.2% precision, and 92.8% F1-score compared to the baseline.

Both CSO and POA independently yielded hyperparameter configurations that enhanced the generalization ability of the YOLOv7 model on the test split, with CSO providing broader search capabilities and POA focusing on adaptive exploitation.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the relatively small size of the dataset, which restricts the final generalization ability of the trained model, and notes that mini-epoch evaluations used for fitness calculation may introduce noise.

Further research is recommended for the Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) class due to its smaller sample size (470 images) compared to Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma, to enhance sensitivity and reduce bias toward larger categories.
important_remarks: The study relies on a relatively small dataset (totaling ~6,421 augmented images), which the authors acknowledge restricts the final generalization ability of the trained model.

Significant class imbalance exists in the original data, with Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) having only 470 images compared to Melanoma (3,460) and BCC (2,491); the paper notes that further research is desirable for the SCC class due to this small sample size.
The optimization process used 'mini-epoch evaluations' to calculate fitness, which the authors admit may introduce noise into the fitness evaluations.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) to validate the performance differences between the baseline and optimized models.

External validation was not performed; the model was evaluated only on a held-out test split from the MED-NODE and BCC-MEL-SCC public datasets, limiting evidence of robustness across different populations or imaging devices.
The study is published in a conference proceedings (ICIIET) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by the use of stratified sampling for dataset splitting, but the reliance on on-the-fly augmentation means the effective dataset size is synthetic, potentially inflating performance metrics if not carefully monitored.
